perturbation.original_text: Owner shall pay Service Provider within thirty (30) days after the invoice date. Fees are conditioned upon timely payment and any past due balance will accrue interest at the monthly rate of one and one half percent (1.5%).
perturbation.changed_text: Owner shall pay Service Provider within sixty (60) days after the invoice date. Fees are conditioned upon timely payment and any past due balance will accrue interest at the monthly rate of three percent (3%).
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the payment timeframe to 60 days and the interest rate to 3% may violate California Prompt Payment Laws which aim to ensure timely payments to subcontractors and may set limits on allowable interest rates.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5
perturbation.law_explanation: California law sets standards for home improvement contracts, including payment schedules. By extending the payment period to 60 days and increasing the interest rate to 3%, this modification could violate provisions ensuring reasonable payment terms and potentially exceeding legal interest rate limits. In particular, Section 7159.5(a)(4) requires the payment schedule to be tied to the contractor's progress and Section 7159.5(a)(5) prohibits finance charges exceeding a certain amount.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment period to 60 days and increases the interest rate to 3%. The original text had a payment period of 30 days and an interest rate of 1.5%. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest this could violate California Prompt Payment Laws and exceed legal interest rate limits. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 7159.5, applies to home improvement contracts and states that failure to comply with the provisions is cause for discipline, which implies that the modifications could be non-compliant. It mentions the agreed contract amount shall exclude finance charges.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Services Commencement Date and remain effective for ten (10) years (the "Initial Term") unless terminated in accordance with its terms. This Agreement shall be subject to an automatic extension for consecutive one (1) year periods thereafter (each, an "Extension Term" and together with the Initial Term, the "Term"), unless terminated (i) in accordance with its terms or (ii) upon thirty (30) days' written notice by either Party to the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Services Commencement Date and remain effective for ten (10) years (the "Initial Term") unless terminated in accordance with its terms. This Agreement shall be subject to an automatic extension for consecutive one (1) year periods thereafter (each, an "Extension Term" and together with the Initial Term, the "Term"), unless terminated (i) in accordance with its terms or (ii) upon five (5) days' written notice by either Party to the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the written notice period for termination to 5 days may be considered unreasonable and could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under California law, especially considering the long initial term and automatic renewal.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2309 addresses the absence of specific time provisions and termination clauses in contracts. While it allows for termination at will if the contract is indefinite, it also mandates that termination requires 'reasonable notification.' Reducing the notice period to five days may be deemed commercially unreasonable, particularly in a long-term service agreement, contradicting the implied requirement for fair notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2309 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 5 days, which contradicts the requirement of 'reasonable notification' as stated in California Commercial Code Section 2309(3). The original text provides a 30-day notice, which is likely considered reasonable for a ten-year agreement with automatic one-year extensions. Reducing this to 5 days is likely unreasonable and unconscionable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable.' The modified text doesn't dispense with notification entirely, but the drastic reduction to 5 days undermines the intent of 'reasonable notification', particularly given the nature of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Within five (5) days after the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and upon Owner's payment in full of the amounts due Service Provider under the Agreement, Service Provider shall deliver to Owner all of the SEF O&M Reports, SEF books, records and property in its possession or under its control, and all materials, supplies, consumables, manuals and any other items furnished to Service Provider by Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: Within ninety (90) days after the termination or expiration of this Agreement, and upon Owner's payment in full of the amounts due Service Provider under the Agreement, Service Provider shall deliver to Owner all of the SEF O&M Reports, SEF books, records and property in its possession or under its control, and all materials, supplies, consumables, manuals and any other items furnished to Service Provider by Owner.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the period for delivering records and property to 90 days after termination might unduly delay the owner's access to critical operational data, potentially violating California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contracts, which requires parties to act reasonably and not hinder the other party's benefits under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1655 states that 'If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed.' While the original five days may be considered reasonable, extending it to ninety days for delivering essential operational records might be deemed unreasonable, particularly if it impedes the owner's ability to continue operating the facility efficiently or comply with regulatory requirements. It may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 5 days to 90 days for delivering records after termination of the agreement introduces a legal contradiction because it may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under California law. The original text specified a reasonable timeframe (5 days). California Civil Code Section 1655 implies stipulations necessary to make a contract reasonable. Extending the delivery timeframe to 90 days could be deemed unreasonable and impede the owner's ability to operate efficiently, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith.
